title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman reports that after 446 abolitionists rallied and 330 testified for the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act (TX HB896), Texas House Judiciary Chair Jeff Leach killed the bill because it would criminalize mothers who obtain abortions. Silberman argues Leach's stance fails on three counts: it leaves DIY abortion legal, it removes the deterrent effect of the law, and it repeats the very inconsistency Justice Blackmun used to justify Roe v. Wade.
audiences: pro-life legislator who opposes criminalizing women

questions.question: What was the immediate political situation that prompted this article?
questions.answer: Hundreds of abolitionists had just packed a marathon Capitol hearing for TX HB896, and only days later Rep. Jeff Leach announced he would refuse to bring the bill to a vote, effectively killing it for the two-year session.
questions.quote: The culture of death was assailed on Monday as 446 abolitionists showed up to the Texas State capitol to support the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act (TX HB896), a bill which would completely abolish abortion in the state. 330 of them testified in favor of the bill, causing the hearing to last until nearly 3 a.m.

questions.question: What reason did Jeff Leach give for blocking TX HB896?
questions.audience: pro-life legislator who opposes criminalizing women
questions.answer: Leach, the Judiciary and Civil Jurisprudence Committee Chair, publicly said the bill went too far because it would expose women who get abortions to criminal liability.
questions.quote: Leach lambasted the bill because it criminalizes abortion by “subject[ing] women who undergo abortions to criminal liability.”

questions.question: Why does Silberman say a bill that punishes only the abortionist is functionally pointless?
questions.answer: Because if mothers and fathers have automatic immunity, self-managed (do-it-yourself) abortion remains legal, and the abortion industry is already shifting in that direction, so the law would simply accelerate the shift instead of stopping abortion.
questions.quote: First, abortion isn’t actually illegal if parents who murder their sons and daughters have automatic immunity. If only the abortionist faces justice, do-it-yourself abortion is legal. And with the abortion industry [moving toward DIY abortion anyway](https://reproaction.org/campaign/self-managed-abortion/), a bill providing legal penalty only for the abortionist accomplishes nothing but expedite the evolution of the abortion industry.

questions.question: How does Jason Storms's personal testimony support the deterrence argument?
questions.answer: Storms told the committee that he had been an accomplice to his own child's abortion in the 1990s and that he and his girlfriend would never have gone through with it had they known they could face homicide charges; the law itself would have saved that child.
questions.quote: “Here’s a fact: my girlfriend and I, if we knew we would’ve been facing homicide charges, would never have aborted that child. That child would be alive today. I’d have a 22-year-old little child that I could celebrate life with right now that’s not here. The law is a deterrent to crime. We shouldn’t think of this only as a matter of putting a woman on the stand. We should think of this as a great deterrent. Men and women would not think of doing this if we stood firm on the law and provided equal protection for these children.”

questions.question: What parental-duty principle does Storms invoke to justify holding parents legally accountable?
questions.answer: He points out that Texas already prosecutes parents for neglect, abuse, and homicide of their born children because parents have a duty to protect their children, and that duty begins at conception, not birth.
questions.quote: “Mothers and fathers – parents – right now in Texas can be charged with parental neglect, parental abuse, and even parental homicide when we see the tragedy of parents taking the lives of their own children. It’s because mothers and fathers have a duty to love and protect their children. That responsibility doesn’t start when they’re born, but it starts when they’re conceived.”

questions.question: How does Silberman tie pre-Roe abortion statutes to the Roe v. Wade decision itself?
questions.answer: He argues that Texas's old anti-abortion law, which exempted the mother from prosecution, gave Justice Harry Blackmun the opening to claim pro-lifers themselves did not really treat the unborn as full persons — an inconsistency Blackmun spelled out in footnote 54 of Roe.
questions.quote: Third, abortion bans which exempted parents from legal penalty are what led to the legalization of abortion in the first place. Dallas District Attorney Henry Wade, who was representing the State of Texas in Roe v Wade, argued that the equal protection clause of the 14th amendment guaranteed preborn human beings equal protection of the law against murder. But the inconsistency of Texas’ anti-abortion laws at the time which didn’t legally penalize mothers for getting abortions provided Justice Harry Blackmun an avenue by which to claim that even pro-life people didn’t really believe abortion was murder. He details this inconsistency in footnote 54 of the Roe v Wade decision.

questions.question: What exactly did Blackmun say in footnote 54 that Silberman quotes?
questions.answer: Blackmun pointed out that Texas treated the woman as neither principal nor accomplice and assigned a far lower penalty for criminal abortion than for murder, which he argued was incoherent if the fetus were truly a person under the Fourteenth Amendment.
questions.quote: “There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out, n. 49, supra, that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?”

questions.question: How does the article respond to the worry that mothers seeking abortions are themselves victims of difficult circumstances?
questions.audience: pro-life legislator who opposes criminalizing women
questions.answer: Silberman concedes that hard circumstances are real and may justify leniency in individual cases, but compares the logic to other crimes — difficult situations don't grant blanket immunity, they're factors for prosecutors, judges, and juries to weigh case by case.
questions.quote: An armed robber might go hungry without the $150 in the till at the corner store. A man might be driven to acts of violence by the physical abuse he suffered as a child. A drunk driver who kills someone might have been drinking because her husband just left her. Such circumstances may warrant a more lenient sentence in some cases, but they do not remove the need for justice to be served.

questions.question: Why does the article frame Leach's decision as a literal death sentence for 110,000 babies?
questions.answer: Because the Texas legislature only meets every two years, killing the bill in 2019 means that roughly 110,000 unborn children will be aborted in Texas before another abolition bill can even be considered.
questions.quote: The Texas legislature meets only every two years, meaning the killing of the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act is a literal and undeniable death sentence for 110,000 preborn human beings. They will die and be cast into landfills, incineration chambers, and down garbage disposals so that Jeff Leach and his allies among pro-life lobbyists and state Republican leaders can virtue signal that they’re not like those mean abolitionists who actually think preborn human beings should get equal justice and protection.
